meta.edition: I (Grammaticus Novus / teacher guide as scanned)

validation.note: LC I teaches ~10 words/lesson; the guide defers 3rd-declension gender to next year; sum/possum are irregular.

meta.note: Rebuilt 2026-07-07 from the LC I Teacher Guide (scanned pp. 31-61: Lesson Plans I-XXV and Review Lessons I-V). Sayings, titles, derivatives, grammar, paradigms, and prayer schedule transcribed from the guide. Vocabulary is complete at 5-lesson-block level (review lesson keys); entries marked placement:'inferred' are block-certain but lesson-position inferred. The guide prints no macrons at scan resolution. Per-lesson vocab sidebars illegible. NOTE: the LC student text is unavailable — the tu-declension (L25) and the future/imperfect 2nd-conj paradigms are standard forms derived from the guide's own rules; history questions are original app content matched to the guide's answer key (item 20 lost at a page break); songs Adeste Fideles and Christus Vincit referenced in guidelines but not scheduled. Previous file preserved as latina-christiana.backup.json. APP MERGE: clip paradigms (puella/servus/amo/video) and Maxey reading_keys carried from pre-rebuild app data by lesson number — reading alignment to the new lesson sequence not yet reviewed.

pronunciation_rules.condition: when followed by i and a vowel

songs.title: Dona Nobis Pacem
songs.note: good first song (guidelines p.11); from the Agnus Dei

songs.title: Adeste Fideles
songs.note: O Come All Ye Faithful; composed in Latin (guidelines p.11)

songs.title: Christus Vincit
songs.note: 'Christ conquers, Christ reigns, Christ rules'; appropriate for Easter (guidelines p.11)

lessons.title: First Conjugation
lessons.focus: First conjugation verbs; definition of a verb

saying.background: Summary of the Rule of St. Benedict, who founded the monastery at Monte Cassino in 530 A.D.; the monasteries preserved faith and civilization through the dark ages.

lessons.grammar: A VERB IS A WORD THAT SHOWS ACTION. Every Latin verb includes a pronoun; the letter o stands for I. First conjugation forms taught by oral recitation; meanings given next week.

practical_latin.source: guide exercise keys

prayer.title: The Our Father (Lord's Prayer)
prayer.latin: Pater Noster, qui es in caelis, sanctificetur nomen Tuum. Adveniat regnum Tuum. Fiat voluntas Tua sicut in caelo et in terra. Panem nostrum cotidianum da nobis hodie, et dimitte nobis debita nostra, sicut et nos dimittimus debitoribus nostris. Et ne nos inducas in tentationem, sed libera nos a malo. Amen.
prayer.english: Our Father, who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread, and forgive us our debts, as we also forgive our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil. Amen.
prayer.note: Daily recitation: Pater Noster and Table Blessing throughout (guide, LP I).

lessons.title: First Conjugation
lessons.focus: Present tense of amo; personal endings; definition of a noun

saying.background: From the Roman historian Florus: the ideals and language of Rome became the source of unity for the Italian peninsula and the Mediterranean world.

lessons.grammar: A NOUN IS A PERSON, PLACE, OR THING. A PRONOUN IS A WORD THAT STANDS FOR A NOUN. Personal endings o, s, t, mus, tis, nt.

practical_latin.source: guide exercise keys

lessons.focus: Conjugating a verb; stem and stem vowel; singular and plural; 1st/2nd/3rd person
lessons.grammar: To CONJUGATE is to write the verb in its different forms. THE STEM IS THE PART OF THE WORD THAT DOESN'T CHANGE; stem vowel a (missing from the first form).

lessons.title: Irregular Verb Sum
lessons.focus: Present tense of sum; Latin numbers 1-10; verb = action or state of being

saying.background: Motto on U.S. coins and the Great Seal; America, like Rome, is a union of different peoples based on ideals.

lessons.grammar: A VERB IS A WORD THAT SHOWS ACTION OR STATE OF BEING. Sum is irregular: the stem changes. It is the most common verb in any language.

lessons.title: First Declension
lessons.focus: First declension case endings; declining a noun; case endings vs personal endings

saying.background: Virgil, greatest of the Roman poets, wrote the Aeneid; Christians in the Middle Ages read his prophecy of a miraculous child as foretelling Christ.

lessons.grammar: WRITING A NOUN IN ALL OF ITS FORMS IS CALLED DECLINING THE NOUN. The endings are CASE ENDINGS. Verbs have personal endings, nouns have case endings.

lessons.title: First Declension, Agreement of Subject and Verb
lessons.focus: Second declension -us nouns introduced; subject + action verb sentences; verb agrees with subject in person and number

saying.english: In the year of Our Lord
saying.background: All time is dated from the Incarnation, the birth of Christ.

lessons.grammar: Model: subject + action verb (Maria vocat). A verb must agree in person and number with its subject. Latin has no words for the articles the and a.

lessons.title: Second Declension, Plural Subjects
lessons.focus: Second declension case endings; nominative case; plural subjects

saying.background: Motto of the United States Marine Corps; often abbreviated Semper Fi.

lessons.grammar: A DECLENSION IS A GROUP OF NOUNS THAT HAVE THE SAME OR SIMILAR CASE ENDINGS. The first horizontal row is the NOMINATIVE CASE, used for the subject, singular or plural.

lessons.title: Second Declension Neuter
lessons.focus: Second declension -um nouns; declension determined by genitive singular

saying.background: Refers especially to the American Civil War, which destroyed the pre-industrial culture of the South.

lessons.grammar: All nouns whose genitive singular ends in i belong to the second declension; all nouns whose genitive singular ends in ae belong to the first declension. Think of a as a plural ending for -um nouns.

lessons.title: Second Declension Neuter, Gender
lessons.focus: Three genders; first declension feminine, second declension -us masculine, -um neuter

saying.background: Motto of the state of New York and of many schools that emphasized Latin and academic excellence.

lessons.grammar: MASCULINE, FEMININE, NEUTER. Neuter case endings compared with masculine; nominative plural in a: Oppidum pugnat / Oppida pugnant.

lessons.title: Adjectives, Singular
lessons.focus: First and second declension adjectives, singular; adjective agrees with noun in gender, number, case

saying.latin: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus, Dominus Deus Sabaoth
saying.english: Holy, Holy, Holy, Lord God of Hosts
saying.background: From Isaiah 6:3 and Revelation 4:8; the Sanctus is one of the oldest elements of the worship liturgy of the church, sung in Gregorian chant.

lessons.grammar: AN ADJECTIVE DESCRIBES (MODIFIES) A NOUN OR PRONOUN. An adjective must match (agree with) its noun in gender, number, and case: magna stella, altus murus, longa via, parvus gladius.

prayer.title: The Sanctus
prayer.latin: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus, Dominus Deus Sabaoth, pleni sunt caeli et terra gloria Tua. Hosanna in excelsis. Benedictus qui venit in nomine Domini. Hosanna in excelsis.
prayer.english: Holy, Holy, Holy, Lord God of Hosts, heaven and earth are full of Thy glory. Hosanna in the highest. Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord. Hosanna in the highest.
prayer.note: Daily recitation: Pater Noster and Table Blessing throughout (guide, LP I).

lessons.title: Adjectives, Plural
lessons.focus: First and second declension adjectives, plural; predicate adjectives with linking verb

saying.background: Motto on the Great Seal, printed on the dollar bill beneath the pyramid and the Eye of Providence.

lessons.grammar: Model: subject + linking verb + predicate adjective (Puella est bona). The PREDICATE ADJECTIVE follows a linking verb but still agrees with the subject: magnae stellae, alti muri, alta valla.

practical_latin.source: app extension (Phaedrus/corpus), pre-rebuild — not in LC I

prayer.title: The Sanctus
prayer.latin: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus, Dominus Deus Sabaoth, pleni sunt caeli et terra gloria Tua. Hosanna in excelsis. Benedictus qui venit in nomine Domini. Hosanna in excelsis.
prayer.english: Holy, Holy, Holy, Lord God of Hosts, heaven and earth are full of Thy glory. Hosanna in the highest. Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord. Hosanna in the highest.
prayer.note: Daily recitation: Pater Noster and Table Blessing throughout (guide, LP I).

lessons.title: Irregular Verb Possum
lessons.focus: Possum = sum with pos-/pot- prefixed; adverbs and prepositions
lessons.grammar: Pot- precedes forms beginning with e; pos- precedes forms beginning with s. An ADVERB modifies a verb; a PREPOSITION shows relationship.

lessons.title: Second Conjugation
lessons.focus: Second conjugation: stem vowel e, present in 1st person singular

saying.english: I came, I saw, I conquered
saying.background: Perhaps the most famous Latin saying: Caesar's dispatch to the Senate after defeating Pharnaces II, King of Pontus, in 47 B.C.

lessons.grammar: Second conjugation stem vowel is e and is not missing from the 1st person singular (porto vs moneo). Find the stem by removing -o; add the personal endings.

prayer.title: The Sanctus
prayer.latin: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus, Dominus Deus Sabaoth, pleni sunt caeli et terra gloria Tua. Hosanna in excelsis. Benedictus qui venit in nomine Domini. Hosanna in excelsis.
prayer.english: Holy, Holy, Holy, Lord God of Hosts, heaven and earth are full of Thy glory. Hosanna in the highest. Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord. Hosanna in the highest.
prayer.note: Daily recitation: Pater Noster and Table Blessing throughout (guide, LP I).

paradigms.label: Second Conjugation Present Tense (video - I see)
paradigms.source: app clip paradigm (pre-rebuild data); audio clips in public/audio

lessons.title: Second Conjugation
lessons.focus: Predicate noun with linking verb (Christus est Deus); more second conjugation practice

saying.latin: Agnus Dei, qui tollis peccata mundi
saying.english: Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world
saying.background: The words of John the Baptist proclaiming the Messiah (John 1:29); like the Sanctus, an ancient part of church worship sung in Gregorian chant.

lessons.grammar: Third sentence model: subject + linking verb + predicate noun. The predicate noun renames the subject and agrees in number and case (nominative); it may differ in gender.

practical_latin.english: The wolf could not seize the lamb.

prayer.english: Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world, have mercy on us. Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world, have mercy on us. Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world, grant us peace.

saying.english: Fools laugh at the Latin language
saying.background: Ovid, best known for the Metamorphoses, was exiled by Augustus, whose moral reforms opposed the decadence Ovid's literature symbolized.

lessons.grammar: Students may confuse habito and habeo, pugna and pugno. Predicate noun sentences with plural subjects: Gladii sunt tela.

lessons.title: First Conjugation Future
lessons.focus: Future tense of first conjugation; six tenses; constellation names (3rd declension -o nouns)

saying.background: Peter's question in John 13:36, 'Quo vadis, Domine?'; also Sienkiewicz's novel of Rome under Nero and the martyrdoms of Peter and Paul.

lessons.grammar: Latin future adds the tense sign bi to the present stem: stem + stem vowel + tense sign + personal ending (loss of i in 1st sg., u in 3rd pl.). Virgo and leo end in -o but are 3rd declension nouns, not verbs.

saying.background: Cardinal Newman: 'A university is an alma mater, knowing her children one by one, not a foundry, or a mint, or a treadmill.'

lessons.grammar: The genitive singular identifies the declension and gives the stem in all five declensions (3rd declension stem = genitive minus -is). Future endings added to the present stem mone-.

saying.background: The peace and prosperity that resulted from Roman law and order imposed on the diverse peoples of the empire.

lessons.grammar: Imperfect tense endings (bam, bas, bat, bamus, batis, bant) will form a new tense next week. Third declension spelling shifts from nominative to genitive follow patterns.

saying.background: In Ephesians 6:11-17, St. Paul uses the armor of the Roman soldier as a metaphor for the spiritual armor of the Christian soldier.

lessons.grammar: Imperfect = imperfect tense endings added to the present stem voca-. Breakdown: stem, stem vowel, tense sign ba, personal ending. The vowel a is in ba and was.

saying.background: A concept associated with democracy: law derives its legitimacy from the people; the vox populi is reported continually in opinion polls.

lessons.grammar: The imperfect of the second conjugation is formed by adding the imperfect tense endings to the present stem mone-. Five review words this week: ira, silva, nuntius, fama, hora.

lessons.title: Personal Pronouns — First Person
lessons.focus: Declension of the 1st person pronoun; Pater Noster translation

saying.background: The ancient custom of making the sign of the cross, practiced by Roman Catholics (left to right) and Eastern Orthodox Christians (right to left), invoking the Trinity: 'In the name of the Father and the Son and the Holy Spirit.'

lessons.grammar: Declension of the 1st person pronouns. Mihi is pronounced miki (an h between two vowels is pronounced k). Exercises translate phrases of the Pater Noster.

prayer.title: The Our Father (Lord's Prayer)
prayer.latin: Pater Noster, qui es in caelis, sanctificetur nomen Tuum. Adveniat regnum Tuum. Fiat voluntas Tua sicut in caelo et in terra. Panem nostrum cotidianum da nobis hodie, et dimitte nobis debita nostra, sicut et nos dimittimus debitoribus nostris. Et ne nos inducas in tentationem, sed libera nos a malo. Amen.
prayer.english: Our Father, who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread, and forgive us our debts, as we also forgive our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil. Amen.
prayer.note: Daily recitation: Pater Noster and Table Blessing throughout (guide, LP I).

paradigms.label: First Person Pronoun: ego / nos
paradigms.source: printed, Lesson Plan XXIV

lessons.title: Personal Pronouns — Second Person
lessons.focus: Declension of the 2nd person pronoun

saying.background: In Shakespeare's play, Caesar's last words; Caesar ceased to resist when he saw his most trusted friend among the conspirators — the classic expression for betrayal.

lessons.grammar: Declension of the 2nd person pronoun 'you'; English meanings are all 'you' in singular and plural ('you all' helps distinguish).

practical_latin.english: You too, Brutus? (Caesar's last words per Shakespeare)

prayer.title: The Our Father (Lord's Prayer)
prayer.latin: Pater Noster, qui es in caelis, sanctificetur nomen Tuum. Adveniat regnum Tuum. Fiat voluntas Tua sicut in caelo et in terra. Panem nostrum cotidianum da nobis hodie, et dimitte nobis debita nostra, sicut et nos dimittimus debitoribus nostris. Et ne nos inducas in tentationem, sed libera nos a malo. Amen.
prayer.english: Our Father, who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread, and forgive us our debts, as we also forgive our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil. Amen.
prayer.note: Daily recitation: Pater Noster and Table Blessing throughout (guide, LP I).

lessons.title: Review Lesson I (Lessons 1–5)

latin_song.title: Dona Nobis Pacem
latin_song.note: Guide (General Teaching Guidelines): sung as a round, 'a good first song to teach in Latin'; from the Agnus Dei.

lessons.history_note: Famous Men of Rome ch. 1-2 tested with this block (guide p. 18 test schedule); see top-level history section

lessons.title: Review Lesson II (Lessons 6–10)
lessons.history_note: Famous Men of Rome ch. 3-5 tested with this block (guide p. 18 test schedule); see top-level history section

lessons.title: Review Lesson III (Lessons 11–15)
lessons.history_note: Famous Men of Rome ch. 6-8 tested with this block (guide p. 18 test schedule); see top-level history section

lessons.title: Review Lesson IV (Lessons 16–20)
lessons.history_note: Famous Men of Rome ch. 9-11 tested with this block (guide p. 18 test schedule); see top-level history section

lessons.title: Review Lesson V (Lessons 21–25)
lessons.history_note: Famous Men of Rome ch. 12-13 tested with this block (guide p. 18 test schedule); see top-level history section

history.source: LC I Teacher Guide, History Guide pp. 17-28; reading text is Famous Men of Rome chs. 1-13

adversaries: Rome's neighbors on the Latium plain (Latins/Albans)
the rich cities of Etruria north of the Tiber
barbarian tribes of the Apennine mountains
the Gauls, who crossed the Alps
the Greek colonies of southern Italy (Magna Graecia)
overview.note: Guide advises telling/reading each story, writing 'words to know' on the board, and assigning history questions as homework; Charlotte Mason-style narration ('tell the story back') recommended. Maps and chart (guide pp. 19-23) are reproducible.

history.questions_source: Questions are ORIGINAL, written for this app (the LC student text is not available); answers and their numbering are from the guide's key, pp. 24-28. Answer #20 fell at a scan page break and is unrecoverable without the guide.

chapters.note: Legendary founding of Rome; the she-wolf; Palatine Hill; city of refuge; the Sabine women; plebeians, patricians, and the Senate. Guide note compares the fratricide to Cain (St. Augustine: human cities founded in blood; only the city of God in love).

answer_key.question: In what year was Rome founded?
Who were the legendary founders of Rome?

answer_key.answer: They were put in a basket instead of being thrown into the Tiber River.
answer_key.question: How did the twin babies escape being killed?
What animal nursed the twins?
On what river was Rome built?

answer_key.answer: They cut off the head of their wicked uncle the king.
answer_key.question: How did the twins take revenge on their wicked uncle?
On which hill did Romulus begin his city?

answer_key.answer: Which brother the city would be named after
answer_key.question: What did the brothers quarrel over?

answer_key.answer: By observing any unusual flight of birds
answer_key.question: How did the brothers agree to settle their dispute?

answer_key.answer: He made it a city of refuge
answer_key.question: How did Romulus attract settlers to his new city?
From what neighboring tribe did the Romans take their wives?

answer_key.answer: The Rape (abduction) of the Sabine Women
answer_key.question: What is that famous abduction called?
What were the common people of Rome called?
What were the noble families called?
What was the council of elders called?

answer_key.answer: The greatest city of the world
answer_key.question: What did Rome eventually become?

chapters.note: Rome's second king; family farm and state religion; the farm as the 'cradle of virtue' (guide cites Jefferson's admiration of the citizen-soldier-farmer). All ancient societies depended on slavery.

answer_key.question: Who was the second king of Rome?
Whose labor did all ancient societies depend on?
What way of life did the Romans consider the cradle of virtue?

illegible: answer 20 falls at the p.24/25 page break in the scan — not captured

chapters.note: War with Alba settled by champions; the three fertile plains (Etruria, Latium, Campania) and the Latins.

answer_key.question: Name the three fertile plains of western Italy.
What people lived on the plain of Latium?
Which two sets of triplet brothers fought as champions for Rome and Alba?

answer_key.answer: twelve miles south of Rome in the Latium plain
answer_key.question: Where was the city of Alba?

chapters.note: Etruscan kings of Rome: Tarquin (Temple of Jupiter on the Capitoline, Circus Maximus, forum), Servius Tullius (census, wall), Tarquin the Proud and the Sibylline books. Etruria's twelve cities; Tuscany preserves the name.

answer_key.question: What rich country lay north of Rome, between the Tiber and Arno rivers?
What port city stood at the mouth of the Tiber?
What was a priest who read the omens called?
On which hill did Tarquin build the Temple of Jupiter?
What were the king's attendants called?

answer_key.answer: The fasces was an ax with a bundle of rods tied around it.
answer_key.question: What was the fasces?
What was Rome's public square called?
What great arena did Tarquin build for races and games?
What counting of the citizens did Servius Tullius begin?
What was a prophetess called?

answer_key.answer: The Sibylline Books were kept in the Capitol, the Temple of Jupiter.
answer_key.question: Where were the Sibylline Books kept?
What is Tarquin Superbus called in English?

chapters.note: Rome expels the Etruscan kings and becomes a republic of free citizens (guide compares America's rebellion against King George); first consuls; Brutus executes his own sons.

answer_key.question: Who led Rome in driving out the kings?
What crime provoked the overthrow of the monarchy?
In what year did the Republic begin?
What were the two magistrates elected each year called?
Which consul had his own sons executed for treason?

chapters.note: Horatius holds the bridge against Lars Porsena's Etruscans; immortalized in Macaulay's poem (a challenging read-aloud for ages 8+).

answer_key.question: What enemy marched on Rome to restore Tarquin?
Who was the Etruscan king who besieged Rome?
Who held the bridge against the Etruscan army?

chapters.note: Gaius Mucius slips into the Etruscan camp, kills the king's secretary by mistake, and thrusts his right hand into the flame; Porsena, hearing 300 more youths had sworn the same oath, makes peace.

answer_key.answer: Gaius Mucius — burned his right hand in the flame to show Roman bravery; Lars Porsena made peace with Rome
answer_key.question: Tell the story of Mucius: how did he show Roman bravery, and what came of it?

chapters.note: War with the Volscians (capital Corioli); debt and usury; plebeians win tribunes with the veto; Coriolanus turns traitor and is turned back by his mother and the women of Rome.

answer_key.answer: The Volscians lived in Latium south of Rome; their capital was Corioli.
answer_key.question: Who were the Volscians, and what was their capital?

answer_key.answer: The patricians loaned the plebeians money; unable to repay, plebeians were jailed and even sold into slavery.
answer_key.question: How did debt bring the plebeians into misery?
What officers did the plebeians win to protect their rights?
What power did the tribunes hold?
To whom could a wronged plebeian appeal?

answer_key.answer: Coriolanus's mother and the other noble women of Rome
answer_key.question: Who finally turned Coriolanus back from attacking Rome?

answer_key.answer: "You have saved Rome, but you have ruined your son."
answer_key.question: What did Coriolanus's mother tell her son?

chapters.note: The Fabii champion justice for the plebeians and fall fighting Veii, the rich Etruscan city.

answer_key.question: Which family offered to carry on the war with Veii alone?
Against which rich Etruscan city did the Fabii fight?

chapters.note: Called from the plow to be dictator against the Aequians; makes the enemy pass under the yoke; refuses a crown and returns to his field.

answer_key.question: Which enemy trapped the Roman army in the hills?
What office was Cincinnatus given, and for how long?
Who was called from his plow to save Rome?

answer_key.answer: By making them "pass under the yoke"
answer_key.question: How did Cincinnatus humble the defeated enemy?

answer_key.answer: He refused to be crowned king, voluntarily gave up his power, and went back to plowing his field.
answer_key.question: How did Cincinnatus show his virtue after the victory?

chapters.note: Veii and Falerii close the Etruscan wars; the Gauls under Brennus sack Rome in 390 B.C. (the 'blackest day'); the Capitol saved by the cackling of geese; Camillus, Second Founder of Rome. Rome would not fall to barbarians again for 800 years.

answer_key.question: Which Roman general finally conquered Veii?
What other Etruscan city did Camillus defeat?
What are the captured goods of war called?

answer_key.answer: Taking more than his share of the spoils of Veii
answer_key.question: What was Camillus accused of?
From what land did the barbarian invaders come?
Who led the Gauls against Rome?
In what year did the Gauls sack Rome?
What stronghold held out against the Gauls?
What sound warned the defenders of the Capitol at night?
Who rescued Rome and was called its Second Founder?

chapters.note: Duel with the giant Gaul (guide notes the David-and-Goliath echo); obedience as the soldier's first duty.

answer_key.question: Who accepted the giant Gaul's challenge to single combat?
What did Manlius Torquatus teach is a soldier's first duty?
Which commander punished even his own son for disobeying orders?

chapters.note: Censor; the Appian Way and aqueducts; Magna Graecia and Tarentum; Pyrrhus and his elephants; the Pyrrhic victory; Cineas; with the Greeks' defeat the unification of Italy is complete.

answer_key.question: What office did Appius Claudius hold?
What structure did Appius build to bring water into Rome?
What famous road did Appius build?
For whom is the Appian Way named?
What did the Romans call the Greek cities of southern Italy?

answer_key.answer: Because they had produced great philosophy, art, and literature, and the Romans had not.
answer_key.question: Why did the Greeks look down on the Romans?
Which Greek city insulted Rome's ambassadors?

answer_key.answer: The Tarentines laughed at the dress (toga) of the Romans and their attempt at speaking Greek.
answer_key.question: How did the Tarentines insult the Romans?

answer_key.answer: "The stain on this toga will be washed out in your blood."
answer_key.question: What did the Roman ambassador answer the Tarentines?
What Greek king came to fight for Tarentum?
What animals did Pyrrhus bring into battle?

answer_key.answer: A victory in which the winner suffers great losses
answer_key.question: What is a 'Pyrrhic victory'?

answer_key.answer: "The tongue of Cineas wins more cities than the sword of Pyrrhus."
answer_key.question: What saying honors the diplomacy of Cineas?
Who persuaded the Senate to refuse peace with Pyrrhus?

pater-noster.title: The Our Father (Lord's Prayer)
pater-noster.latin: Pater Noster, qui es in caelis, sanctificetur nomen Tuum. Adveniat regnum Tuum. Fiat voluntas Tua sicut in caelo et in terra. Panem nostrum cotidianum da nobis hodie, et dimitte nobis debita nostra, sicut et nos dimittimus debitoribus nostris. Et ne nos inducas in tentationem, sed libera nos a malo. Amen.
pater-noster.english: Our Father, who art in heaven, hallowed be Thy name. Thy kingdom come. Thy will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Give us this day our daily bread, and forgive us our debts, as we also forgive our debtors. And lead us not into temptation, but deliver us from evil. Amen.
pater-noster.note: Guide, Lesson Plan I: recite daily with the Table Blessing from Lesson 1. Text from Prima Latina (same publisher).

table-blessing.title: The Table Blessing
table-blessing.latin: Benedic, Domine, nos et haec Tua dona, quae de Tua largitate sumus sumpturi, per Christum Dominum nostrum. Amen.
table-blessing.english: Bless us, O Lord, and these Thy gifts, which from Thy bounty we are about to receive, through Christ our Lord. Amen.
table-blessing.note: Recited daily from Lesson 1 per guide. Text from Prima Latina (same publisher).

sanctus.title: The Sanctus
sanctus.latin: Sanctus, Sanctus, Sanctus, Dominus Deus Sabaoth, pleni sunt caeli et terra gloria Tua. Hosanna in excelsis. Benedictus qui venit in nomine Domini. Hosanna in excelsis.
sanctus.english: Holy, Holy, Holy, Lord God of Hosts, heaven and earth are full of Thy glory. Hosanna in the highest. Blessed is He who comes in the name of the Lord. Hosanna in the highest.
sanctus.note: Lesson 13 saying is its opening line. Text from Prima Latina (same publisher).

oremus.title: Oremus
oremus.note: Appears in Lesson 16 exercise key ('Let us pray'). Text from Prima Latina.

agnus-dei.title: Agnus Dei
agnus-dei.latin: Agnus Dei, qui tollis peccata mundi, miserere nobis. Agnus Dei, qui tollis peccata mundi, miserere nobis. Agnus Dei, qui tollis peccata mundi, dona nobis pacem.
agnus-dei.english: Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world, have mercy on us. Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world, have mercy on us. Lamb of God, who takes away the sins of the world, grant us peace.
agnus-dei.note: Lesson 17 saying is its opening line; full liturgical text retained from previous file — verify against student text.

gloria-patri.title: Gloria Patri
gloria-patri.latin: Gloria Patri et Filio et Spiritui Sancto, sicut erat in principio et nunc et semper et in saecula saeculorum. Amen.
gloria-patri.english: Glory be to the Father, and to the Son, and to the Holy Spirit, as it was in the beginning, is now, and ever shall be, world without end. Amen.
gloria-patri.note: Not referenced in the LC I teacher guide; retained as app extension.

ave-maria.title: Ave Maria (Hail Mary)
ave-maria.latin: Ave Maria, gratia plena, Dominus tecum. Benedicta tu in mulieribus, et benedictus fructus ventris tui, Iesus. Sancta Maria, Mater Dei, ora pro nobis peccatoribus, nunc et in hora mortis nostrae. Amen.
ave-maria.english: Hail Mary, full of grace, the Lord is with thee. Blessed art thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of thy womb, Jesus. Holy Mary, Mother of God, pray for us sinners, now and at the hour of our death. Amen.
ave-maria.note: Not referenced in the LC I teacher guide; retained as app extension.

credo.title: Credo (Apostles' Creed — opening)
credo.latin: Credo in Deum, Patrem omnipotentem, Creatorem caeli et terrae. Et in Iesum Christum, Filium eius unicum, Dominum nostrum.
credo.english: I believe in God, the Father almighty, Creator of heaven and earth. And in Jesus Christ, his only Son, our Lord.
credo.note: Not referenced in the LC I teacher guide; retained as app extension.
